FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), N=9429, ages 11-12 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone as primary exposure. Paper focuses on cyberbullying victimization/perpetration with no separate quantifiable social media measure as qualifying exposure. Abstract states 'Total screen time, particularly on the internet and social media, was associated with both cyberbullying victimization and perpetration' but cyberbullying is the dependent variable, not SM the independent variable. Applied B3.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple logistic regression analyses with AOR, 95% CI reported for sociodemographic factors predicting cyberbullying victimization/perpetration (Tables 2-3). Adjusted associations between screen time modalities and cyberbullying outcomes reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: AOR with 95% CI for all associations reported in Tables 2-3 (e.g., males AOR 0.80 95% CI 0.68-0.94 for victimization; sexual minorities AOR 2.83 95% CI 1.69-4.75 for victimization; screen time OR 1.11 95% CI 1.08-1.14)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data; cross-sectional regression analyses with inferential statistics

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate identified; published peer-reviewed study

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable. Cyberbullying is the dependent variable (outcome), not SM. SM appears only as a covariate/predictor. Does not meet FT-EC7 condition (a) which requires SM to be the dependent variable in EVERY analysis.
notes: Paper excluded because cyberbullying is the outcome of interest, not SM/smartphone use. The inclusion of screen time/SM as predictors in the adjusted models does not satisfy FT-IC2 which requires SM/smartphone as the primary exposure/independent variable with a non-SM outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, baseline (2016-2018), N=10,755, ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying contemporary screen time modalities measured as independent variables: television, videos (YouTube), video games, social networking, texting, video chat. Social networking is explicitly stated as qualifying SM variable. Applied B1 - separate β coefficients reported for each modality.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses estimating associations between sociodemographic factors and 6 contemporary forms of screen time. Tables III and IV report B coefficients with 95% CI for all modalities.

FT_IC4.evidence: Linear regression B coefficients (beta values) with 95% CI reported in Tables III and IV for all screen time modalities vs sociodemographic factors (e.g., Black children 1.58 more hours 95% CI 1.35-1.79; male 0.75 hours more 95% CI 0.63-0.89)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study baseline data; cross-sectional multiple linear regression analyses

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate identified; published peer-reviewed study in Journal of Pediatrics 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 APPLIES. All three conditions met: (a) Screen time/SM are DEPENDENT variables in all analyses - outcomes predicted by sociodemographic factors; (b) Every predictor is demographic-only: sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education - all in closed demographic set; (c) Analysis is cross-sectional baseline data (not longitudinal/prospective). This is a sociodemographic → SM study with NO SM → health outcome analysis. Does not trigger cross-sectional carve-out (B15) as applied to demographics.
notes: Study predicts screen time use FROM sociodemographic characteristics only. No health outcomes measured. Fits FT-EC7 profile exactly.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, December 2020 COVID-19 survey, N=4,106, ages 12-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM and internet variables measured as independent variables: frequency of checking COVID-related social media (Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat), internet/YouTube use. Social media explicitly specified as qualifying variable. Applied B1 - separate coefficients for SM vs internet vs TV modalities.

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression with risk ratios (RR) reported. Table 2 shows associations between COVID information sources (SM, internet, TV, parental counseling) and 'always masking' behavior with 95% CI.

FT_IC4.evidence: Risk ratios (RR) and adjusted risk ratios (ARR) with 95% CI reported in Table 2 for all information sources predicting masking (e.g., SM RR 1.15 95% CI 1.07-1.25 unadjusted, ARR 1.09 95% CI 1.00-1.19 adjusted; parental counseling ARR 1.28 95% CI 1.20-1.36)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD COVID-19 survey data; modified Poisson regression analyses with outcome (masking behavior)

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplicate identified; published peer-reviewed study in Preventive Medicine Reports 2022

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. While SM is measured as independent variable predicting masking (condition a partially met), the model includes multiple non-demographic predictors: parental counseling on masking importance (parenting behavior/intervention), television media exposure, internet exposure. These are not in the closed demographic-only set per condition (b). Parental counseling (parenting) is explicitly excluded by B17 as disqualifying predictor.
notes: Study examines SM and other information sources predicting COVID masking behavior. Includes parental counseling (parenting variable) which triggers B17 exclusion from FT-EC7. SM is independent variable with health behavior outcome (masking), not a demographic-only study.
